addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Note this is specifically the machine type for the **Cloud Build private worker pool** (used to build vessels), not a general VM. That constraint matters for your single-CPU question, so let me verify the supported set rather than answer from general GCE knowledge.

Based on the search results, I found information about Google Cloud Build private worker pools and supported machine types. The machineType field specifies the Compute Engine machine type for your private pool instance. If you don't specify a value, then Cloud Build uses the default value of e2-medium. Otherwise, specify one of the following machine types with availability varying by region.

The search results reference a table showing the availability of machine types by region, but the actual detailed list of all supported machine types is not fully displayed in the search results. However, the documentation indicates that both e2 and n1 series are supported for private pools:

- The default machine type is e2-medium
- If left blank, Cloud Build uses the default value of e2-standard-2

Examples shown in the documentation include:
- e2-medium
- e2-standard-2
- e2-standard-4

For a complete and current list of supported machine types including all e2 and n1 variants available for Cloud Build private worker pools, you should refer to the official Google Cloud documentation at the "Private pool configuration file schema" page, which contains the full table of supported machine types organized by region.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
## What RBRD dictates now

`rbmm_moorings/rbrd.env:32` → `e2-standard-2` = **2 vCPU / 8 GB**. This is the machine type for the **Cloud Build private worker pool**, not a general-purpose VM. That's the binding constraint: Cloud Build private pools only accept a subset of Compute Engine machine types (the E2 and N1 families), not the whole GCE catalog. The pool default if unset is `e2-medium`.

## Machine types available for the private pool

**E2 family** (cost-optimized — what you're using):

| Type | vCPU | Memory |
|------|------|--------|
| `e2-medium` | 2 (shared-core) | 4 GB |
| `e2-standard-2` ← current | 2 | 8 GB |
| `e2-standard-4 / -8 / -16 / -32` | 4–32 | 4 GB/vCPU |
| `e2-highcpu-8 / -16 / -32` | 8–32 | 1 GB/vCPU |
| `e2-highmem-2 / -4 / -8 / -16` | 2–16 | 8 GB/vCPU |

**N1 family** (older, also accepted):

| Type | vCPU | Memory |
|------|------|--------|
| `n1-standard-1` | **1** | 3.75 GB |
| `n1-standard-2 … -96` | 2–96 | 3.75 GB/vCPU |
| `n1-highcpu-2 …`, `n1-highmem-2 …` | 2+ | varies |

(The wider GCE universe — N2, N2D, C2, C3, C4, T2D, M-series, etc. — exists but is **not** offered for Cloud Build private pools, so it's irrelevant for RBRD.)

## Single CPU, not double?

**Yes — `n1-standard-1` is the one genuine single-vCPU option** in the supported set.

Two caveats specific to this repo:

1. **E2 shared-core types are a trap.** `e2-micro`/`e2-small`/`e2-medium` look small but all report **2 vCPUs** (just throttled shares of a core). There is no 1-vCPU E2.

2. **Your quota-preflight parser assumes the trailing number is the vCPU count.** `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:200` does `z_vcpus="${RBRD_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE##*-}"`. So `n1-standard-1` parses cleanly to `1`, but `e2-medium` would parse to `medium` and trip the "Cannot parse vCPU count… skipping quota preflight" warning at line 203. If you ever want a 1-vCPU pool, `n1-standard-1` is both the only real single-CPU choice *and* the one that keeps the preflight working.

Sources: [Create and manage private pools](https://cloud.google.com/build/docs/private-pools/create-manage-private-pools), [Private pool configuration file schema](https://docs.cloud.google.com/build/docs/private-pools/private-pool-config-file-schema)

message.content: Do you think n1 cpus are more expensive than e2?

content.text: Short answer: **yes, N1 is generally more expensive than E2 for equivalent vCPU/RAM.** E2 is explicitly Google's *cost-optimized* family and was launched as the cheaper successor to N1. But let me pull current per-hour numbers rather than rely on the general claim.

input.query: e2-standard-2 vs n1-standard-2 vs n1-standard-1 hourly price Google Cloud 2026

content.content:
Web search results for query: "e2-standard-2 vs n1-standard-2 vs n1-standard-1 hourly price Google Cloud 2026"

Links: [{"title":"Google Cloud Pricing Calculator 2026: GCP Cost Estimator ...","url":"https://cdcalculators.com/gcp-calculator/"},{"title":"How to Optimize Google Compute Engine Pricing (2026 Guide)","url":"https://www.usage.ai/blogs/gcp/compute-engine/"},{"title":"Cloud Build pricing | Google Cloud","url":"https://cloud.google.com/build/pricing"},{"title":"Cloud Build pricing update | Google Cloud","url":"https://cloud.google.com/build/pricing-update"},{"title":"e2-standard-2 pricing and specs - Google Cloud | Holori","url":"https://calculator.holori.com/gcp/vm/e2-standard-2"},{"title":"e2-standard-2 pricing: $48.9183 monthly","url":"https://www.economize.cloud/resources/gcp/pricing/compute-engine/e2-standard-2/"},{"title":"General Purpose VM pricing | Google Cloud","url":"https://cloud.google.com/products/compute/pricing/general-purpose"},{"title":"Google Cloud Pricing Review 2025: Plans & Costs Explained - Tekpon 2026","url":"https://tekpon.com/software/google-cloud/pricing/"},{"title":"e2-standard-2 specs and pricing | GCP | CloudPrice","url":"https://cloudprice.net/gcp/compute/instances/e2-standard-2"},{"title":"VM instance pricing | Google Cloud","url":"https://cloud.google.com/compute/vm-instance-pricing"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comparison of the hourly prices for these Google Cloud machine types in 2026:

## Hourly Pricing Comparison

**e2-standard-2**: $0.0670 per hour in us-central1 region

**n1-standard-2**: The search results don't contain specific pricing for n1-standard-2. However, they indicate that N1 instances are an older generation and generally more expensive than E2 instances.

**n1-standard-1**: $0.0475 per hour, suitable for small-scale applications

## Key Takeaway

The e2-standard-2 ($0.067/hr) is more expensive than the n1-standard-1 ($0.0475/hr), but this is partly because it offers 2 vCPUs and 8GB of memory compared to n1-standard-1's 1 vCPU. For specific n1-standard-2 pricing, you may want to check Google Cloud's official pricing calculator directly, as the search results focused more on E2 and N2 instance types for 2026.

results:
Based on the search results, here's a comparison of the hourly prices for these Google Cloud machine types in 2026:

## Hourly Pricing Comparison

**e2-standard-2**: $0.0670 per hour in us-central1 region

**n1-standard-2**: The search results don't contain specific pricing for n1-standard-2. However, they indicate that N1 instances are an older generation and generally more expensive than E2 instances.

**n1-standard-1**: $0.0475 per hour, suitable for small-scale applications

## Key Takeaway

The e2-standard-2 ($0.067/hr) is more expensive than the n1-standard-1 ($0.0475/hr), but this is partly because it offers 2 vCPUs and 8GB of memory compared to n1-standard-1's 1 vCPU. For specific n1-standard-2 pricing, you may want to check Google Cloud's official pricing calculator directly, as the search results focused more on E2 and N2 instance types for 2026.

content.text:
Comparing properly requires separating **per-vCPU rate** from **absolute cost**, and they point in opposite directions.

**Per vCPU (on-demand, us-central1):**

| Type | vCPU / RAM | $/hr | $/vCPU-hr |
|------|-----------|------|-----------|
| `e2-standard-2` (current) | 2 / 8 GB | ~$0.067 | **~$0.034** |
| `n1-standard-1` | 1 / 3.75 GB | ~$0.0475 | **~$0.0475** |
| `n1-standard-2` | 2 / 7.5 GB | ~$0.095 | ~$0.0475 |

So **per vCPU, N1 is ~40% more expensive than E2** — exactly as you'd expect, since E2 is the cost-optimized family that replaced N1. Your instinct is right.

**But the thing you'd actually be comparing** isn't per-vCPU — it's "should I drop the build pool to a single CPU." There, `n1-standard-1` at **$0.0475/hr is cheaper in absolute terms** than `e2-standard-2` at **$0.067/hr**, because it's half the cores. You'd pay ~29% less per build-minute… and builds would run on 1 slower vCPU instead of 2, so wall-clock build time likely rises enough to eat the savings (Cloud Build private pools bill on VM time while the build runs).

So the honest read:
- **If you want cheapest-per-unit-compute:** stay on E2. N1 is the wrong family — strictly worse $/vCPU.
- **If you genuinely want a 1-vCPU pool** (you asked earlier): `n1-standard-1` is the *only* supported single-vCPU option, and it is cheaper per hour — but it's a slow, old-generation core, and the savings are probably illusory once build time stretches.

There's no single-vCPU E2, so "single CPU" and "cheapest per vCPU" are mutually exclusive in the supported set. If the goal is lowering build cost, the better levers are usually region choice or keeping E2 and not the core count.

Sources: [VM instance pricing](https://cloud.google.com/compute/vm-instance-pricing), [General Purpose VM pricing](https://cloud.google.com/products/compute/pricing/general-purpose), [Cloud Build pricing](https://cloud.google.com/build/pricing)

lastPrompt: Do you think n1 cpus are more expensive than e2?

aiTitle: List Google Cloud machine types and CPU options

message.content: grep for 32 in this codebase for a memo about our findings with different cpu sizes.  Or grep vCPU, that might work.  32 cores only cut time by half, right?

input.description: Find memo discussing vCPU/core size findings

content.text: The first one is clearly it. Let me read it.

content.content:
1	# Cloud Build machineType A/B — highcpu-32 vs standard-2
2	
3	Pace: ₢BOAAA (highcpu-build-substep-bench), heat ₣BO (rbk-11-mvp-tactical).
4	Bench window: 2026-05-16 17:00 UTC → 2026-05-17 03:35 UTC.
5	
6	## Decision
7	
8	`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` reverts from `e2-highcpu-32` to `e2-standard-2`,
9	locked a priori. This memo documents what was given up, so a future
10	"should we go back?" decision has direct evidence rather than guesswork.
11	
12	## Methodology
13	
14	Three phases. The same 11-dispatch sequence in each, in the same order
15	(sequential — never parallel; BUK fixture discipline plus shared-pool
16	state assumptions):
17	
18	  - rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter conjure ×2 (warmup + data; biggest CPU)
19	  - rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge conjure ×2 (multi-arch + apt-get + cargo)
20	  - rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap conjure ×3 (multi-arch FROM forge)
21	  - rbev-bottle-plantuml bind ×1 (skopeo mirror — registry-I/O reference)
22	  - rbev-graft-demo about+vouch ×1 (mixed mode reference)
23	  - rbev-busybox conjure ×1 (tiny single-stage reference)
24	  - tt/rbw-fV batch vouch ×1 (sweeps all hallmarks)
25	
26	Phase 1 (e2-highcpu-32): existing `canest2bhl100011` depot, pools
27	provisioned at levy time with e2-highcpu-32 (96 vCPU quota grant).
28	
29	Phase 2 (`rbrr.env` flipped to e2-standard-2): same depot, same warm
30	pool. No relevy. Intent: confirm ₢BLAAA's structural finding empirically.
31	
32	Phase 3 (real e2-standard-2 pool): new depot `canest2bhl100012` levied
33	via the gauntlet's `canonical-establish` fixture under
34	`rbrr.env=e2-standard-2`. Original 100011 depot left intact. Onboarding
35	work done via the gauntlet's `onboarding-sequence` fixture (covers 7 of
36	the 11 workloads' first runs as fixture-warmups); remaining data runs
37	dispatched manually in the same order as Phases 1 & 2.
38	
39	Wall-clock per operation = timestamp delta between the opening
40	`rbfd_ordain` event (`Building vessel image` for conjures; `Loaded vessel`
41	for mirror/about+vouch) and the matching terminal `SUCCESS` line, read
42	from the per-tabtarget self-logs in `../logs-buk/`.
43	
44	## Carried over from ₢BLAAA — empirically reconfirmed
45	
46	**machineType is pool-time, not build-time.** `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` is
47	consumed only by the worker-pool create step inside `rbgp_depot_levy`
48	(`rbgp_Payor.sh:895`/`:921`); per-build dispatch reads nothing from
49	rbrr.env about machine size. Existing pools retain whatever machineType
50	they were provisioned with at levy. Phase 2's wall-clocks land within
51	5–15 seconds of Phase 1's per workload — well inside cloud-build
52	scheduling noise — confirming that the rbrr.env flip alone is a no-op
53	until the next depot is levied.
54	
55	## Per-workload results
56	
57	All 33 dispatches (11 × 3 phases). `build` column means conjure for
58	conjure vessels, mirror for bind, about+vouch for graft, GAR enumeration
59	for batch vouch sweep.
60	
61	| # | Workload | P1 build | P1 vouch | P2 build | P2 vouch | P3 build | P3 vouch |
62	|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
63	| 1 | jupyter conjure (warmup) | 8m39s | 1m02s | 8m49s | 0m55s | 16m52s | 1m49s |
64	| 2 | jupyter conjure (data)   | 8m34s | 0m59s | 8m55s | 0m56s | 15m55s | 1m38s |
65	| 3 | forge conjure (warmup)   | 3m08s | 0m59s | 3m16s | 1m01s | 6m13s  | 1m52s |
66	| 4 | forge conjure (data)     | 3m16s | 0m57s | 3m16s | 0m59s | 6m29s  | 1m42s |
67	| 5 | airgap conjure (warmup)  | 4m21s | 0m53s | 3m47s | 0m54s | 5m33s  | 1m48s |
68	| 6 | airgap conjure (data 1)  | 3m46s | 0m54s | 4m00s | 0m58s | 6m47s  | 2m12s |
69	| 7 | airgap conjure (data 2)  | 3m58s | 0m59s | 4m02s | 1m04s | 6m54s  | 1m38s |
70	| 8 | plantuml bind (mirror)   | 1m26s | 0m58s | 1m27s | 0m50s | 2m07s  | 1m38s |
71	| 9 | graft-demo (about+vouch) | —     | 1m36s | —     | 1m31s | —      | 2m21s |
72	| 10| busybox conjure          | 1m27s | 1m05s | 1m27s | 1m00s | 2m09s  | 1m37s |
73	| 11| batch vouch sweep        | —     | 0m33s | —     | 1m05s | —      | 0m31s |
74	
75	Phases 1 & 2 differ from Phase 3 in pool machineType; Phases 1 & 2 share
76	the same warm highcpu-32 pool.
77	
78	## Speedup ledger (highcpu-32 over standard-2)
79	
80	Warmup runs discarded where >1 data run was collected. Data-run averages.
81	
82	| Workload category | HC32 avg | S2 avg | HC32 speedup |
83	|---|---|---|---|
84	| jupyter conjure (CPU-heavy single-stage) | 8m45s (n=2) | 15m55s (n=1) | **1.82×** |
85	| forge conjure (multi-arch + apt + cargo) | 3m16s (n=2) | 6m29s (n=1) | **1.98×** |
86	| airgap conjure (multi-arch FROM forge)   | 3m56s (n=4) | 6m51s (n=2) | **1.74×** |
87	| plantuml bind (skopeo mirror)            | 1m27s (n=2) | 2m07s (n=1) | **1.46×** |
88	| graft about+vouch (mixed mode)           | 1m34s (n=2) | 2m21s (n=1) | **1.50×** |
89	| busybox conjure (tiny single-stage)      | 1m27s (n=2) | 2m09s (n=1) | **1.48×** |
90	| vouch tool (per-hallmark)                | 0m58s avg   | 1m45s avg   | **1.81×** |
91	| batch vouch sweep (GAR enumeration)      | 0m49s avg   | 0m31s        | **0.63×** (no-op; network/scale-bound, not CPU) |
92	
93	## Profile groupings
94	
95	**CPU-bound conjures (jupyter, forge):** ~2× slower on standard-2. Forge
96	hits 1.98× — close to the 2-vCPU-versus-32-vCPU ceiling for the parts of
97	the build that actually parallelize (cargo compile, apt installs).
98	
99	**Multi-arch FROM-cached conjures (airgap):** 1.74× slower. The FROM
100	layer (forge image) is cached in GAR, so airgap's incremental work is
101	the airgap-specific Dockerfile only. The CPU shift bites less because
102	some of the build cost is image pulling and push from the registry,
103	which is network-bound regardless of machineType.
104	
105	**Registry-I/O operations (plantuml mirror, busybox conjure,
106	graft about+vouch):** 1.46–1.50× slower. These spend most of their
107	wall-clock on skopeo copy / image push / GAR API calls. CPU is a
108	secondary factor; network is dominant.
109	
110	**Vouch tool:** 1.81× slower. SLSA attestation processing is CPU-bound
111	and runs on a single worker; tracks the conjure ratio.
112	
113	**GAR enumeration (batch vouch sweep):** Network-bound, not CPU. Phase
114	3's 0m31s is actually faster than Phase 1's 0m33s — within noise; not a
115	real effect of machineType.
116	
117	## Cross-host reference (downgraded)
118	
119	May 14 cerebro clean-gauntlet `fO` logs lived at
120	`../logs-buk/hist-rbw-fO-sh-20260514-2*.txt` on cerebro under
121	`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-standard-2` on a cerebro-host-provisioned
122	depot. Originally planned as the primary 2-cpu reference; demoted to
123	cross-host sanity check because Phase 3 provides a same-host
124	standard-2 measurement on the same dispatcher binary version, free of
125	cross-host clock-skew confounds.
126	
127	## Decision implications
128	
129	For workloads dominated by registry-I/O (mirror, busybox, graft), the
130	speedup penalty is modest (~1.5×) and standard-2 is comfortably
131	adequate. For CPU-bound multi-arch conjures, standard-2 roughly doubles
132	wall-clock. The release-qualification gauntlet's whole-pipeline cost on
133	standard-2 is therefore ~1.7× the highcpu-32 cost; this is the kind of
134	budget the locked decision absorbs in exchange for the smaller vCPU
135	quota footprint.
136	
137	If a future "should we go back?" question arises (e.g., per-developer
138	build throughput becomes a constraint), this ledger gives concrete
139	per-workload speedup numbers to plug into the trade-off.
140	
141	## Findings surfaced during the bench
142	
143	**Google's fresh-project Cloud Build vCPU quota has dropped.** Past
144	canest depots got the historical 10-vCPU baseline (encoded in the stale
145	`rbrr.env` comment `"fits 5 concurrent in 10-CPU quota"` and
146	`rbhpq_quota_build.sh` prose). `canest2bhl100012` was levied at
147	`e2-standard-2` and received only **2 vCPU** of
148	`concurrent_private_pool_build_cpus`. Past gauntlets succeeded because
149	the pre-bench `rbrr.env` always carried `e2-highcpu-32` at levy time,
150	for which Google grants 96 vCPU. This was the first canest2 levy in the
151	standard-2 era under the new low-default-quota regime. Required a manual
152	quota-request through the Cloud Console (6 vCPU was the bump granted; 6
153	clears the `RBRR_GCB_MIN_CONCURRENT_BUILDS=3` × 2-vCPU minimum). Stale
154	"10-CPU quota" wording in `rbrr.env` history and `rbhpq_quota_build.sh`
155	should be updated.
156	
157	**Gauntlet has a latent precondition on a non-standard-2 levy.** The
158	`onboarding-sequence` fixture wedges on the jupyter conjure (which needs
159	3 concurrent multi-arch children) when the depot was freshly levied at
160	e2-standard-2 with default quota. Pace ₢BOAAI (post-levy-quota-bump-flow)
161	is slated to design a tighter integration of the quota-bump procedure
162	into levy completion — the levy's degenerate build primes the Cloud
163	Console "Edit Quotas" UI on the freshly-levied project, so the operator
164	can act immediately rather than discovering the wall on first ordain.
165	
166	## Ancillary yields from the pace
167	
168	These weren't the bench, but landed during it:
169	
170	- **`fc179e39f`**: split `zrbfd_registry_preflight`'s airgap empty-anchor
171	  branch to discriminate hallmark-pin vs upstream-enshrine origins. The
172	  diagnostic now steers the operator to the correct recovery path
173	  (canonical handbook track for hallmark-pin, enshrine path for
174	  upstream).
175	- **`f2073e4f4`**: added a wrong-vs-right example block to the gazette
176	  H1-as-delimiter warning in `jjk-claude-context.md`, after hitting the
177	  failure mode mid-pace.
178	- **`25a54c77d`**: added the "absolute paths to working trees" bullet to
179	  JJK's docket anti-patterns list.
180	- **`d7145e0c0`**: retrofitted `Tools/rbk/rbtd/` for three RCG sections
181	  (Output Discipline, Constant Discipline, String Boundary Discipline)
182	  surfaced as cleanup debt during prior pace work.
183	- **`00d99794b`**: anchored theurge's BURV sandbox under BUK
184	  dispatch-provided `BURD_TEMP_DIR`/`BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`.
185	
186	## State at memo close
187	
188	`canest2bhl100011` is the active production depot
189	(`RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=canest2bhl100011`, `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32`).
190	`canest2bhl100012` remains alive in the cloud, unused; operator unmakes
191	it later when expressly chosen.
192

file.content:
# Cloud Build machineType A/B — highcpu-32 vs standard-2

Pace: ₢BOAAA (highcpu-build-substep-bench), heat ₣BO (rbk-11-mvp-tactical).
Bench window: 2026-05-16 17:00 UTC → 2026-05-17 03:35 UTC.

## Decision

`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` reverts from `e2-highcpu-32` to `e2-standard-2`,
locked a priori. This memo documents what was given up, so a future
"should we go back?" decision has direct evidence rather than guesswork.

## Methodology

Three phases. The same 11-dispatch sequence in each, in the same order
(sequential — never parallel; BUK fixture discipline plus shared-pool
state assumptions):

  - rbev-bottle-anthropic-jupyter conjure ×2 (warmup + data; biggest CPU)
  - rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge conjure ×2 (multi-arch + apt-get + cargo)
  - rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap conjure ×3 (multi-arch FROM forge)
  - rbev-bottle-plantuml bind ×1 (skopeo mirror — registry-I/O reference)
  - rbev-graft-demo about+vouch ×1 (mixed mode reference)
  - rbev-busybox conjure ×1 (tiny single-stage reference)
  - tt/rbw-fV batch vouch ×1 (sweeps all hallmarks)

Phase 1 (e2-highcpu-32): existing `canest2bhl100011` depot, pools
provisioned at levy time with e2-highcpu-32 (96 vCPU quota grant).

Phase 2 (`rbrr.env` flipped to e2-standard-2): same depot, same warm
pool. No relevy. Intent: confirm ₢BLAAA's structural finding empirically.

Phase 3 (real e2-standard-2 pool): new depot `canest2bhl100012` levied
via the gauntlet's `canonical-establish` fixture under
`rbrr.env=e2-standard-2`. Original 100011 depot left intact. Onboarding
work done via the gauntlet's `onboarding-sequence` fixture (covers 7 of
the 11 workloads' first runs as fixture-warmups); remaining data runs
dispatched manually in the same order as Phases 1 & 2.

Wall-clock per operation = timestamp delta between the opening
`rbfd_ordain` event (`Building vessel image` for conjures; `Loaded vessel`
for mirror/about+vouch) and the matching terminal `SUCCESS` line, read
from the per-tabtarget self-logs in `../logs-buk/`.

## Carried over from ₢BLAAA — empirically reconfirmed

**machineType is pool-time, not build-time.** `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` is
consumed only by the worker-pool create step inside `rbgp_depot_levy`
(`rbgp_Payor.sh:895`/`:921`); per-build dispatch reads nothing from
rbrr.env about machine size. Existing pools retain whatever machineType
they were provisioned with at levy. Phase 2's wall-clocks land within
5–15 seconds of Phase 1's per workload — well inside cloud-build
scheduling noise — confirming that the rbrr.env flip alone is a no-op
until the next depot is levied.

## Per-workload results

All 33 dispatches (11 × 3 phases). `build` column means conjure for
conjure vessels, mirror for bind, about+vouch for graft, GAR enumeration
for batch vouch sweep.

| # | Workload | P1 build | P1 vouch | P2 build | P2 vouch | P3 build | P3 vouch |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | jupyter conjure (warmup) | 8m39s | 1m02s | 8m49s | 0m55s | 16m52s | 1m49s |
| 2 | jupyter conjure (data)   | 8m34s | 0m59s | 8m55s | 0m56s | 15m55s | 1m38s |
| 3 | forge conjure (warmup)   | 3m08s | 0m59s | 3m16s | 1m01s | 6m13s  | 1m52s |
| 4 | forge conjure (data)     | 3m16s | 0m57s | 3m16s | 0m59s | 6m29s  | 1m42s |
| 5 | airgap conjure (warmup)  | 4m21s | 0m53s | 3m47s | 0m54s | 5m33s  | 1m48s |
| 6 | airgap conjure (data 1)  | 3m46s | 0m54s | 4m00s | 0m58s | 6m47s  | 2m12s |
| 7 | airgap conjure (data 2)  | 3m58s | 0m59s | 4m02s | 1m04s | 6m54s  | 1m38s |
| 8 | plantuml bind (mirror)   | 1m26s | 0m58s | 1m27s | 0m50s | 2m07s  | 1m38s |
| 9 | graft-demo (about+vouch) | —     | 1m36s | —     | 1m31s | —      | 2m21s |
| 10| busybox conjure          | 1m27s | 1m05s | 1m27s | 1m00s | 2m09s  | 1m37s |
| 11| batch vouch sweep        | —     | 0m33s | —     | 1m05s | —      | 0m31s |

Phases 1 & 2 differ from Phase 3 in pool machineType; Phases 1 & 2 share
the same warm highcpu-32 pool.

## Speedup ledger (highcpu-32 over standard-2)

Warmup runs discarded where >1 data run was collected. Data-run averages.

| Workload category | HC32 avg | S2 avg | HC32 speedup |
|---|---|---|---|
| jupyter conjure (CPU-heavy single-stage) | 8m45s (n=2) | 15m55s (n=1) | **1.82×** |
| forge conjure (multi-arch + apt + cargo) | 3m16s (n=2) | 6m29s (n=1) | **1.98×** |
| airgap conjure (multi-arch FROM forge)   | 3m56s (n=4) | 6m51s (n=2) | **1.74×** |
| plantuml bind (skopeo mirror)            | 1m27s (n=2) | 2m07s (n=1) | **1.46×** |
| graft about+vouch (mixed mode)           | 1m34s (n=2) | 2m21s (n=1) | **1.50×** |
| busybox conjure (tiny single-stage)      | 1m27s (n=2) | 2m09s (n=1) | **1.48×** |
| vouch tool (per-hallmark)                | 0m58s avg   | 1m45s avg   | **1.81×** |
| batch vouch sweep (GAR enumeration)      | 0m49s avg   | 0m31s        | **0.63×** (no-op; network/scale-bound, not CPU) |

## Profile groupings

**CPU-bound conjures (jupyter, forge):** ~2× slower on standard-2. Forge
hits 1.98× — close to the 2-vCPU-versus-32-vCPU ceiling for the parts of
the build that actually parallelize (cargo compile, apt installs).

**Multi-arch FROM-cached conjures (airgap):** 1.74× slower. The FROM
layer (forge image) is cached in GAR, so airgap's incremental work is
the airgap-specific Dockerfile only. The CPU shift bites less because
some of the build cost is image pulling and push from the registry,
which is network-bound regardless of machineType.

**Registry-I/O operations (plantuml mirror, busybox conjure,
graft about+vouch):** 1.46–1.50× slower. These spend most of their
wall-clock on skopeo copy / image push / GAR API calls. CPU is a
secondary factor; network is dominant.

**Vouch tool:** 1.81× slower. SLSA attestation processing is CPU-bound
and runs on a single worker; tracks the conjure ratio.

**GAR enumeration (batch vouch sweep):** Network-bound, not CPU. Phase
3's 0m31s is actually faster than Phase 1's 0m33s — within noise; not a
real effect of machineType.

## Cross-host reference (downgraded)

May 14 cerebro clean-gauntlet `fO` logs lived at
`../logs-buk/hist-rbw-fO-sh-20260514-2*.txt` on cerebro under
`RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-standard-2` on a cerebro-host-provisioned
depot. Originally planned as the primary 2-cpu reference; demoted to
cross-host sanity check because Phase 3 provides a same-host
standard-2 measurement on the same dispatcher binary version, free of
cross-host clock-skew confounds.

## Decision implications

For workloads dominated by registry-I/O (mirror, busybox, graft), the
speedup penalty is modest (~1.5×) and standard-2 is comfortably
adequate. For CPU-bound multi-arch conjures, standard-2 roughly doubles
wall-clock. The release-qualification gauntlet's whole-pipeline cost on
standard-2 is therefore ~1.7× the highcpu-32 cost; this is the kind of
budget the locked decision absorbs in exchange for the smaller vCPU
quota footprint.

If a future "should we go back?" question arises (e.g., per-developer
build throughput becomes a constraint), this ledger gives concrete
per-workload speedup numbers to plug into the trade-off.

## Findings surfaced during the bench

**Google's fresh-project Cloud Build vCPU quota has dropped.** Past
canest depots got the historical 10-vCPU baseline (encoded in the stale
`rbrr.env` comment `"fits 5 concurrent in 10-CPU quota"` and
`rbhpq_quota_build.sh` prose). `canest2bhl100012` was levied at
`e2-standard-2` and received only **2 vCPU** of
`concurrent_private_pool_build_cpus`. Past gauntlets succeeded because
the pre-bench `rbrr.env` always carried `e2-highcpu-32` at levy time,
for which Google grants 96 vCPU. This was the first canest2 levy in the
standard-2 era under the new low-default-quota regime. Required a manual
quota-request through the Cloud Console (6 vCPU was the bump granted; 6
clears the `RBRR_GCB_MIN_CONCURRENT_BUILDS=3` × 2-vCPU minimum). Stale
"10-CPU quota" wording in `rbrr.env` history and `rbhpq_quota_build.sh`
should be updated.

**Gauntlet has a latent precondition on a non-standard-2 levy.** The
`onboarding-sequence` fixture wedges on the jupyter conjure (which needs
3 concurrent multi-arch children) when the depot was freshly levied at
e2-standard-2 with default quota. Pace ₢BOAAI (post-levy-quota-bump-flow)
is slated to design a tighter integration of the quota-bump procedure
into levy completion — the levy's degenerate build primes the Cloud
Console "Edit Quotas" UI on the freshly-levied project, so the operator
can act immediately rather than discovering the wall on first ordain.

## Ancillary yields from the pace

These weren't the bench, but landed during it:

- **`fc179e39f`**: split `zrbfd_registry_preflight`'s airgap empty-anchor
  branch to discriminate hallmark-pin vs upstream-enshrine origins. The
  diagnostic now steers the operator to the correct recovery path
  (canonical handbook track for hallmark-pin, enshrine path for
  upstream).
- **`f2073e4f4`**: added a wrong-vs-right example block to the gazette
  H1-as-delimiter warning in `jjk-claude-context.md`, after hitting the
  failure mode mid-pace.
- **`25a54c77d`**: added the "absolute paths to working trees" bullet to
  JJK's docket anti-patterns list.
- **`d7145e0c0`**: retrofitted `Tools/rbk/rbtd/` for three RCG sections
  (Output Discipline, Constant Discipline, String Boundary Discipline)
  surfaced as cleanup debt during prior pace work.
- **`00d99794b`**: anchored theurge's BURV sandbox under BUK
  dispatch-provided `BURD_TEMP_DIR`/`BURD_OUTPUT_DIR`.

## State at memo close

`canest2bhl100011` is the active production depot
(`RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER=canest2bhl100011`, `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE=e2-highcpu-32`).
`canest2bhl100012` remains alive in the cloud, unused; operator unmakes
it later when expressly chosen.

content.text:
Found it: `Memos/memo-20260516-cloudbuild-machinetype-bench.md` — a deliberate A/B of `e2-highcpu-32` (32 vCPU) vs `e2-standard-2` (2 vCPU).

**Your recollection is right — and it's actually a more striking result than "half."** Going from 2 → 32 vCPU is a **16× increase in cores**, and the best-case speedup was only ~2×:

| Workload | Speedup from 32 cores |
|----------|----------------------|
| forge conjure (most CPU-bound) | **1.98×** ← the ceiling |
| jupyter conjure | 1.82× |
| vouch (SLSA, single worker) | 1.81× |
| airgap conjure (multi-arch, FROM-cached) | 1.74× |
| graft / busybox / plantuml (registry-I/O) | 1.46–1.50× |
| batch vouch sweep | 0.63× (network-bound — no benefit) |
| **whole gauntlet** | **~1.7×** |

So "32 cores cut time roughly in half" is the **upper bound**, hit only by the genuinely CPU-parallel builds (cargo compile, apt). Most of the pipeline did far worse than half because it's network/registry-I/O-bound, where vCPU count barely matters.

The reason 16× cores → ~2× wall-clock: each Docker build step is largely serial, and only sub-steps that parallelize (compiles, package installs) can use the extra cores. The forge build hitting 1.98× means it was already near the parallelizable ceiling for those phases — the other ~30 cores sat idle.

That's exactly why the memo locked the revert to `e2-standard-2`: ~1.7× slower whole-pipeline in exchange for a much smaller vCPU quota footprint.

**One staleness flag:** the memo refers to `RBRR_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` / `rbrr.env`, but the live regime now uses `RBRD_GCB_MACHINE_TYPE` / `rbrd.env` (the repo-vs-depot regime rename). The mechanism it documents — *machineType is consumed only at pool-levy time, not per-build* — still holds; only the variable name moved.

message.content: This makes me wonder how much is network latency, which may mean that n1 is cheaper
This makes me wonder how much is network latency, which may mean that n1 is cheaper overall?  Worth testing?

content.text:
Good instinct, and the existing bench actually lets us answer most of it *without* a new test — let me reason it through, because I think the data already points to "no."

**The cost model.** Private pools bill on VM wall-clock. So:
- `n1-standard-1`: ~$0.0475/hr, **1 vCPU, 3.75 GB**
- `e2-standard-2`: ~$0.067/hr, 2 vCPU, 8 GB

n1 wins *only if* it doesn't run more than `0.067/0.0475 ≈ 1.41×` slower. Under 1.41× → cheaper; over → more expensive *and* slower.

**What the bench implies for each profile:**

- **I/O-bound work** (plantuml, busybox, graft): these gained only 1.46–1.5× going from 2→**32** cores, meaning 2 cores is already past the knee — they're network-bound. Dropping 2→1 should barely hurt. **n1 likely wins ~29% here.** Your network-latency hypothesis is correct *for these.*

- **CPU-bound conjures** (jupyter, forge): these scaled ~1.8–2.0× *up* to 32 cores, so the 2nd core is genuinely working. Halving to 1 vCPU plausibly approaches ~2× slower → n1 ends up **~40% more expensive *and* 2× slower.**

**The catch that decides it:** the CPU-bound conjures are also the *longest* jobs by far. In Phase 3 the two jupyter builds were ~16 min each — they dominate total gauntlet wall-clock. n1 saves pennies on the short I/O jobs and loses big on the long CPU jobs. **Net, the gauntlet would almost certainly cost *more* on n1, not less.**

**And a hard risk, not just speed:** n1-standard-1 has **3.75 GB RAM vs 8 GB**. Multi-arch + cargo + apt builds (forge, airgap) may simply OOM. That's a build *failure*, not a slowdown — and it's the most likely reason a test ends early.

**My recommendation:** not worth a full A/B. A full test isn't cheap either — machineType is pool-time, so it requires levying a fresh depot, which (per this same memo) trips the new low-default quota wedge and manual console bumps.

If you still want certainty, the *cheap decisive probe* isn't the whole gauntlet — it's a single question: **does the jupyter conjure even survive 3.75 GB on n1-standard-1, and what's its wall-clock?** That one data point settles both the OOM risk and the worst-case speedup. Want me to estimate the total-cost delta from the existing Phase 3 numbers first, so you can see the margin before deciding whether even that probe is worth it?

message.content: Do you think an e2-standard-4 would be 2x faster then?

content.text:
No — I'd estimate **~1.3–1.4× at best**, and here's why the bench data lets us pin that down rather than guess.

We effectively have two measured points (2 vCPU and 32 vCPU), which is enough to fit a simple Amdahl split (fixed serial part + parallelizable part) and interpolate the 4-vCPU point.

**Forge conjure** (the most CPU-bound, 1.98× at 32 cores):
- T(2) = 389s, T(32) = 196s → solves to ~184s serial + ~411s parallel
- T(4) = 184 + 411/4 ≈ **286s → ~1.36× faster** than standard-2

**Jupyter conjure** (1.82× at 32 cores):
- T(2) = 955s, T(32) = 525s → ~496s serial + ~917s parallel
- T(4) = 496 + 917/4 ≈ **726s → ~1.32× faster**

The reason it's not 2×: even the most parallel build here had a large *serial* floor (~half the 2-core time) that more cores can't touch. Doubling 2→4 only halves the parallel half once — that's the ~1.35× ceiling. I/O-bound builds (plantuml, busybox, graft) would gain even less, near 1×, since they barely moved even at 32 cores.

**The cost sting — this is the real takeaway:** `e2-standard-4` is **2× the hourly rate** of `e2-standard-2` but only ~1.35× faster. So per build it costs roughly `2 / 1.35 ≈ 1.5× more`. It's the same trap as the n1 question, just inverted: you'd be paying 50% more for a 35% speedup. If the goal is throughput-at-any-cost, `e2-standard-4` shaves a third off the long conjures; if the goal is cost, it's strictly worse than standard-2.

Caveat: this is a two-point extrapolation, so treat the 1.35× as ±, not exact. But the *shape* is solid — the 2-vs-32 spread already proves the parallel fraction is limited, and that's what caps any intermediate size. The genuinely uncertain region isn't 4 cores; it's whether any specific build has a parallel burst (a wide cargo or apt phase) that briefly uses more — which would only show up as a spiky benefit, not a sustained 2×.

message.content: are you sure two points extrapoate a line

content.text:
You're right to push — I overstated it. Two points don't pin down a curve; they pin down the *parameters* of whatever curve you assume, with zero residual left over to tell you the shape was right.

Two points determine a line (slope + intercept). But I didn't fit a line — I fit Amdahl's form `T(c) = S + P/c`, which also has exactly two parameters, so it *also* passes through both points perfectly. "Passes through both points" tells you nothing — any two-parameter model does, and they disagree wildly in between:

**Forge, same two points (T(2)=389s, T(32)=196s), three different 2-param fits:**

| Assumed shape | Predicted T(4) | Speedup vs 2-vCPU |
|---|---|---|
| Straight line in cores | ~376s | **~1.03×** |
| Power law `a·c^-k` | ~328s | **~1.19×** |
| Amdahl `S + P/c` | ~286s | **~1.36×** |

Same data, answers from ~1.0× to ~1.4×. With zero degrees of freedom there's no residual, no goodness-of-fit, nothing to reject any of them. So my "the bench data lets us pin that down" was wrong — the data constrains the *endpoints*, not the *4-vCPU value*.

What I *can* defend:
- 4 vCPU is **interpolation** (between 2 and 32), which is safer than extrapolation — whatever the true curve, T(4) is bounded between T(2) and T(32), so it's somewhere in 1.0×–1.98×.
- Amdahl is the most physically motivated model for "fixed serial overhead + parallelizable compute," so my best *single guess* is still ~1.3–1.4× — but that's a modeling prior, not a measurement.

The only thing that actually resolves it is a third data point: one forge conjure on an `e2-standard-4` pool. That's a far cheaper probe than the n1 question (no RAM/OOM risk — standard-4 has 16 GB), and it would both confirm the curve shape and tell you whether the cost-per-build crossover lands where the Amdahl model claims. If you want a real number instead of a model, that's the test worth running.
